'''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。
[[江戸]][[深川 (江東区)|深川]]の[[質屋]]の次男として生まれる。幼時に[[漢学]]や書画を学び、[[寛政]]3年に叔母猪飼氏の[[養子]]となって[[篠山藩]]に仕えるが8年後に[[致仕]]する。その後、2度の結婚に失敗後に[[京橋]]に住まいを構え、[[篆刻]]と[[合巻]]・[[読本]]執筆を業とした。
[[文化 (日本)|文化]]4年（[[1807年]]）に出された『復讐妹背山物語』や[[天保]]6年（[[1835年]]）の『昔模様娘評判記』が代表作であるが、ライフワークになった『大晦日曙草子』（天保10年（[[1839年]]）開始）や『教草女房形気』（[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]））など、婦女子を対象とした平易で温雅な作風に様々な教訓を盛り込んだ話を得意とした。また弘化4年（[[1847年]]）に書かれた[[随筆]]『歴代女装考』は[[近世]]風俗考証の上で貴重な史料となっている。90歳で亡くなるまで積極的に執筆活動を行った。
{{DEFAULTSORT:さんとうきようさん}} [[Category:1769年生]] [[Category:1858年没]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。
[[江戸]][[深川 (江東区)|深川]]の[[質屋]]の次男として生まれる。幼時に[[漢学]]や書画を学び、[[寛政]]3年に叔母[[猪飼氏]]の[[養子]]となって[[篠山藩]]に仕えるが8年後に[[致仕]]する。その後、2度の結婚に失敗後に[[京橋 (東京都中央区)|京橋]]に住まいを構え、[[篆刻]]と[[合巻]]・[[読本]]執筆を業とした。
[[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年（[[1807年]]）に出された『復讐妹背山物語』や[[天保]]6年（[[1835年]]）の『昔模様娘評判記』が代表作であるが、ライフワークになった『大晦日曙草子』（天保10年（[[1839年]]）開始）や『教草女房形気』（[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]））など、婦女子を対象とした平易で温雅な作風に様々な教訓を盛り込んだ話を得意とした。また弘化4年（[[1847年]]）に書かれた[[随筆]]『歴代女装考』は[[近世]]風俗考証の上で貴重な史料となっている。90歳で亡くなるまで積極的に執筆活動を行った。
[[鈴木牧之]]『[[北越雪譜]]』の刊行にあたり「京山人百樹」名義で同書の刪定（添削・修正）に当たったことでも知られる。
{{デフォルトソート:さんとう きようさん}} [[Category:1769年生]] [[Category:1858年没]] [[Category:戯作者]] [[Category:武蔵国の人物]] [[Category:篠山藩の人物]] {{Japanese-history-stub}}

[[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|『[[北越雪譜]]』二編 巻一（[[鈴木牧之]]著、天保12年（1841年）刊）]] [[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年（[[1807年]]）に出された『復讐妹背山物語』や[[天保]]6年（[[1835年]]）の『昔模様娘評判記』が代表作であるが、ライフワークになった『大晦日曙草子』（天保10年（[[1839年]]）開始）や『朧月猫草帋』([[天保]]13年([[1842年]] ) - [[嘉永]]2年( [[1849年]]))[[歌川国芳]]絵 <ref>『朧月猫草帋 7編28巻』{{Cite book|NCID=BB04697476|ref=harv}}また、 [[2013年]]に[[金子信久]]訳で『おこまの大冒険〜朧月猫の草紙〜』{{ISBN2|978-4756244291}}復刻</ref>、『教草女房形気』（[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]））など、婦女子を対象とした平易で温雅な作風に様々な教訓を盛り込んだ話を得意とした。また弘化4年（[[1847年]]）に書かれた[[随筆]]『歴代女装考』は[[近世]]風俗考証の上で貴重な史料となっている。90歳で亡くなるまで積極的に執筆活動を行った。
[[鈴木牧之]]『[[北越雪譜]]』の刊行にあたり「京山人百樹」名義で同書の刪定（添削・修正）に当たったことでも知られる。
== 外部リンク == * {{青空文庫著作者|1932|山東 京山}} * [http://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html 山東京山] - [[東京文化財研究所]]
{{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:さんとう きようさん}} [[Category:戯作者]] [[Category:武蔵国の人物]] [[Category:篠山藩の人物]] [[Category:1769年生]] [[Category:1858年没]]

{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。
== 略歴 == [[江戸]][[深川 (江東区)|深川]]の[[質屋]]の次男として生まれる。幼時に[[漢学]]や書画を学び、[[寛政]]3年に叔母[[猪飼氏]]の[[養子]]となって[[篠山藩]]に仕えるが8年後に[[致仕]]する。その後、2度の結婚に失敗後に[[京橋 (東京都中央区)|京橋]]に住まいを構え、[[篆刻]]と[[合巻]]・[[読本]]執筆を業とした。
[[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|『[[北越雪譜]]』二編 巻一（[[鈴木牧之]]著、天保12年（1841年）刊）]] [[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年（[[1807年]]）に出された『復讐妹背山物語』や[[天保]]6年（[[1835年]]）の『昔模様娘評判記』が代表作であるが<ref>{{kotobank|2=デジタル大辞泉の解説}}参照</ref> <ref>高木元 訳 [[歌川国貞]]、[[歌川国貞 (3代目)]]、[[歌川国輝 (2代目)]]、[[歌川芳綱]]、歌川国清 絵『山東京山伝奇小説集 (江戸怪異綺想文芸大系)』2003年 {{ISBN2|978-4336042743}}[[国書刊行会]]発行「復讐妹背山物語」「昔模様娘評判記」等が収録</ref>、ライフワークになった『大晦日曙草子』（天保10年（[[1839年]]）開始）や『朧月猫草帋』(天保13年([[1842年]] ) - [[嘉永]]2年( [[1849年]]))[[歌川国芳]]絵 <ref>『朧月猫草帋 7編28巻』{{Cite book|NCID=BB04697476|ref=harv}}また、 [[2013年]]に[[金子信久]]訳で復刻『おこまの大冒険〜朧月猫の草紙〜』{{ISBN2|978-4756244291}}パイインターナショナル発行</ref>、『教草女房形気』（[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]））など、婦女子を対象とした平易で温雅な作風に様々な教訓を盛り込んだ話を得意とした。また弘化4年（[[1847年]]）に書かれた[[随筆]]『歴代女装考』は[[近世]]風俗考証の上で貴重な史料となっている。90歳で亡くなるまで積極的に執筆活動を行った。 [[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|山東京山の墓]] [[鈴木牧之]]『[[北越雪譜]]』の刊行にあたり「京山人百樹」名義で同書の刪定（添削・修正）に当たったことでも知られる。
== 外部リンク == * {{青空文庫著作者|1932|山東 京山}} * [http://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html 山東京山] - [[東京文化財研究所]] ==脚注== {{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:さんとう きようさん}} [[Category:戯作者]] [[Category:武蔵国の人物]] [[Category:篠山藩の人物]] [[Category:1769年生]] [[Category:1858年没]]

{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。 [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『かゞ見やま 草履恥の段』山東京山文､[[歌川国芳]]画]]
== 略歴 == [[江戸]][[深川 (江東区)|深川]]の[[質屋]]の次男として生まれる。幼時に[[漢学]]や書画を学び、[[寛政]]3年に叔母[[猪飼氏]]の[[養子]]となって[[篠山藩]]に仕えるが8年後に[[致仕]]する。その後、2度の結婚に失敗後に[[京橋 (東京都中央区)|京橋]]に住まいを構え、[[篆刻]]と[[合巻]]・[[読本]]執筆を業とした。 [[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『道行 猫柳婬月影』山東京山文、歌川国芳画]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|『[[北越雪譜]]』二編 巻一（[[鈴木牧之]]著、天保12年（1841年）刊）]] [[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年（[[1807年]]）に出された『復讐妹背山物語』や[[天保]]6年（[[1835年]]）の『昔模様娘評判記』が代表作であるが<ref>{{kotobank|2=デジタル大辞泉の解説}}参照</ref> <ref>高木元 訳 [[歌川国貞]]、[[歌川国貞 (3代目)]]、[[歌川国輝 (2代目)]]、[[歌川芳綱]]、歌川国清 絵『山東京山伝奇小説集 (江戸怪異綺想文芸大系)』2003年 {{ISBN2|978-4336042743}}[[国書刊行会]]発行「復讐妹背山物語」「昔模様娘評判記」等が収録</ref>、ライフワークになった『大晦日曙草子』（天保10年（[[1839年]]）開始）や『朧月猫草帋』(天保13年([[1842年]] ) - [[嘉永]]2年( [[1849年]]))[[歌川国芳]]絵 <ref>『朧月猫草帋 7編28巻』{{Cite book|NCID=BB04697476|ref=harv}}また、 [[2013年]]に[[金子信久]]訳で復刻『おこまの大冒険〜朧月猫の草紙〜』{{ISBN2|978-4756244291}}パイインターナショナル発行</ref>、『教草女房形気』（[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]））など、婦女子を対象とした平易で温雅な作風に様々な教訓を盛り込んだ話を得意とした。また弘化4年（[[1847年]]）に書かれた[[随筆]]『歴代女装考』は[[近世]]風俗考証の上で貴重な史料となっている。90歳で亡くなるまで積極的に執筆活動を行った。 [[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|山東京山の墓]] [[鈴木牧之]]『[[北越雪譜]]』の刊行にあたり「京山人百樹」名義で同書の刪定（添削・修正）に当たったことでも知られる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * {{青空文庫著作者|1932|山東 京山}} * [https://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html 山東京山] - [[東京文化財研究所]]
{{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:さんとう きようさん}} [[Category:戯作者]] [[Category:武蔵国の人物]] [[Category:篠山藩の人物]] [[Category:1769年生]] [[Category:1858年没]]

{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。 [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『かゞ見やま 草履恥の段』山東京山文､[[歌川国芳]]画]]
== 略歴 == [[江戸]][[深川 (江東区)|深川]]の[[質屋]]の次男として生まれる。幼時に[[漢学]]や書画を学び、[[寛政]]3年に叔母[[猪飼氏]]の[[養子]]となって[[篠山藩]]に仕えるが8年後に[[致仕]]する。その後、2度の結婚に失敗後に[[京橋 (東京都中央区)|京橋]]に住まいを構え、[[篆刻]]と[[合巻]]・[[読本]]執筆を業とした。 [[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『道行 猫柳婬月影』山東京山文、歌川国芳画]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|『[[北越雪譜]]』二編 巻一（[[鈴木牧之]]著、天保12年（1841年）刊）]] [[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年（[[1807年]]）に出された『復讐妹背山物語』や[[天保]]6年（[[1835年]]）の『昔模様娘評判記』が代表作であるが<ref>{{kotobank|2=デジタル大辞泉の解説}}参照</ref> <ref>高木元 訳 [[歌川国貞]]、[[歌川国貞 (3代目)]]、[[歌川国輝 (2代目)]]、[[歌川芳綱]]、歌川国清 絵『山東京山伝奇小説集 (江戸怪異綺想文芸大系)』2003年 {{ISBN2|978-4336042743}}[[国書刊行会]]発行「復讐妹背山物語」「昔模様娘評判記」等が収録</ref>、ライフワークになった『大晦日曙草子』（天保10年（[[1839年]]）開始）や『朧月猫草帋』(天保13年([[1842年]] ) - [[嘉永]]2年( [[1849年]]))[[歌川国芳]]絵 <ref>『朧月猫草帋 7編28巻』{{Cite book|NCID=BB04697476|ref=harv}}また、 [[2013年]]に[[金子信久]]訳で復刻『おこまの大冒険〜朧月猫の草紙〜』{{ISBN2|978-4756244291}}パイインターナショナル発行</ref>、『教草女房形気』（[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]））など、婦女子を対象とした平易で温雅な作風に様々な教訓を盛り込んだ話を得意とした。また弘化4年（[[1847年]]）に書かれた[[随筆]]『歴世女装考』は[[近世]]風俗考証の上で貴重な史料となっている。90歳で亡くなるまで積極的に執筆活動を行った。 [[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|山東京山の墓]] [[鈴木牧之]]『[[北越雪譜]]』の刊行にあたり「京山人百樹」名義で同書の刪定（添削・修正）に当たったことでも知られる。

{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。 [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『かゞ見やま 草履恥の段』山東京山文､[[歌川国芳]]画]]

revision.text:
'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Aozora Bunko Author|1932|Santō Kyōzan}} * [https://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html Santō Kyōzan] - Tokyo Cultural Property Research Institute

[[ja:山東京山]]

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

[[Category:New pages]][[ja:山東京山]]

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Aozora Bunko Author|1932|Santō Kyōzan}} * [https://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html Santō Kyōzan] - Tokyo Cultural Property Research Institute

[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]

''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Aozora Bunko Author|1932|Santō Kyōzan}} * [https://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html Santō Kyōzan] - Tokyo Cultural Property Research Institute

In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Aozora Bunko Author|1932|Santō Kyōzan}} * [https://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html Santō Kyōzan] - Tokyo Cultural Property Research Institute

''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. revision.comment: /* 略歴 */ 死去時を追記
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。 [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『かゞ見やま 草履恥の段』山東京山文､[[歌川国芳]]画]]
== 略歴 == [[江戸]][[深川 (江東区)|深川]]の[[質屋]]の次男として生まれる<ref>{{Cite web |title=山東京伝｜国史大辞典・日本国語大辞典・日本大百科全書・世界大百科事典｜ジャパンナレッジ |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc NetAdvance |last=Inc}}</ref>。幼時に[[漢学]]や書画を学び、[[寛政]]3年に叔母[[猪飼氏]]の[[養子]]となって[[篠山藩]]に仕えるが8年後に[[致仕]]する<ref>*{{Cite book|和書 |author=津田眞弓 |authorlink= |title=山東京山年譜稿 |publisher=ぺりかん社 |year=2006 |page=16,23 |isbn=4831510661 |ref={{SfnRef|津田眞弓 |2006}}}}</ref>。その後、2度の結婚に失敗後に[[京橋 (東京都中央区)|京橋]]に住まいを構え、[[篆刻]]と[[合巻]]・[[読本]]執筆を業とした。 [[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『道行 猫柳婬月影』山東京山文、歌川国芳画]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|『[[北越雪譜]]』二編 巻一（[[鈴木牧之]]著、天保12年（1841年）刊）]] [[文化 (元号)|文化]]4年（[[1807年]]）に出された『復讐妹背山物語』や[[天保]]6年（[[1835年]]）の『昔模様娘評判記』が代表作であるが<ref>{{kotobank|2=デジタル大辞泉の解説}}参照</ref> <ref>高木元 訳 [[歌川国貞]]、[[歌川国貞 (3代目)]]、[[歌川国輝 (2代目)]]、[[歌川芳綱]]、歌川国清 絵『山東京山伝奇小説集 (江戸怪異綺想文芸大系)』2003年 {{ISBN2|978-4336042743}}[[国書刊行会]]発行「復讐妹背山物語」「昔模様娘評判記」等が収録</ref>、ライフワークになった『大晦日曙草子』（天保10年（[[1839年]]）開始）や『朧月猫草帋』(天保13年([[1842年]] ) - [[嘉永]]2年( [[1849年]]))[[歌川国芳]]絵 <ref>『朧月猫草帋 7編28巻』{{Cite book|NCID=BB04697476|ref=harv}}また、 [[2013年]]に[[金子信久]]訳で復刻『おこまの大冒険〜朧月猫の草紙〜』{{ISBN2|978-4756244291}}パイインターナショナル発行</ref>、『教草女房形気』（[[弘化]]3年（[[1846年]]））など、婦女子を対象とした平易で温雅な作風に様々な教訓を盛り込んだ話を得意とした。また弘化4年（[[1847年]]）に書かれた[[随筆]]『歴世女装考』は[[近世]]風俗考証の上で貴重な史料となっている。晩年まで積極的に執筆活動を行ったが、1858年（安政5年）、江戸で多くの死者を出した[[コレラ]]に感染して死去<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=池田正一郎 |title=日本災変通志 |publisher=新人物往来社 |year=2004-12-15 |page=694 |isbn=4-404-03190-4 }}</ref>。90歳。 [[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|山東京山の墓]] [[鈴木牧之]]『[[北越雪譜]]』の刊行にあたり「京山人百樹」名義で同書の刪定（添削・修正）に当たったことでも知られる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * {{青空文庫著作者|1932|山東 京山}} * [https://www.tobunken.go.jp/materials/banduke_name/748351.html 山東京山] - [[東京文化財研究所]]
{{Normdaten}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:さんとう きようさん}} [[Category:戯作者]] [[Category:武蔵国の人物]] [[Category:篠山藩の人物]] [[Category:1769年生]] [[Category:1858年没]]

{{出典の明記|date=2016年11月}} '''山東 京山'''（さんとう きょうざん、[[明和]]6年[[6月15日 (旧暦)|6月15日]]（[[1769年]][[7月18日]]）－[[安政]]5年[[9月24日 (旧暦)|9月24日]]（[[1858年]][[10月30日]]））は、[[江戸時代]]後期の[[戯作者]]。本名は岩瀬 百樹（いわせ ももき）。[[字]]は鉄梅。号は覧山・涼仙。[[山東京伝]]は兄。 [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|流行猫の戯『かゞ見やま 草履恥の段』山東京山文､[[歌川国芳]]画]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seppu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seppu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seppu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.

[[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Santō Kyōzan]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:山東京山]]

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seqqu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seqqu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seqqu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.

Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seqqu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seqqu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seqqu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seqqu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seqqu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seqqu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seqqu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

revision.text:
'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seqqu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seqqu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seqqu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seqqu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seqqu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seqqu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."

'''Santō Kyōzan''' (山東 京山, June 15, 1769 (Meiwa 6) – October 30, 1858 (Ansei 5, 24th day of the 9th month)) was a late Edo period {{ill|gesaku writer|ja|戯作者}}. His real name was Iwase Momoki (岩瀬 百樹), and his courtesy name was Tetsubai. He also used the pen names Ranzan and Ryōsen. He was the younger brother of {{ill|Santō Kyōden|ja|山東京伝}}. [[File:(Kagamiyama) zori-hachi no dan (BM 1998,0218,0.22).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Kagamiyama Zōri Hachi no Dan'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]] (art)]]
== Biography == Santō Kyōzan was born as the second son of a pawnbroker in {{ill|Fukagawa|ja|深川 (江東区)}}, Edo.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Santō Kyōden｜National Histories Dictionary・Japanese Language Dictionary・Comprehensive Japanese Encyclopedia・World Encyclopedia｜JapanKnowledge |url=https://japanknowledge.com/introduction/keyword.html?i=398 |website=JapanKnowledge |access-date=2023-09-23 |first=NetAdvance Inc. |last=Inc.}}</ref> As a child, he studied classical Chinese literature, calligraphy, and painting. In 1791 (Kansei 3), he became the adopted son of his aunt, the wife of the {{ill|Inukai family|ja|猪飼氏}}, and entered the service of the Sasayama Domain, but he retired eight years later.<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |author=Mayumi Tsuda |title=Santō Kyōzan Nenpukō |publisher=Perikan Publishing |year=2006 |pages=16, 23 |isbn=4831510661}}</ref> After two failed marriages, he settled in {{ill|Kyōbashi|ja|京橋 (東京都中央区)}}, making a living through {{ill|tenkoku|ja|篆刻}} (seal carving) and writing {{ill|gōkan|ja|合巻}} and {{ill|yomihon|ja|読本}}.
[[File:(Michiyuki) nekoyanagi sakari no tsukikage (BM 1998,0218,0.21).jpg|thumb|200px|Flowery Cats Play, ''Michiyuki Nekoyanagi no Tsukikage'' by Santō Kyōzan (text) and Utagawa Kuniyoshi (art)]] [[File:Hokuetsu Seqqu Part 2 volume 1.jpg|thumb|160px|''{{ill|Hokuetsu Seqqu|ja|北越雪譜}}'' Volume Two, written by {{ill|Suzuki Bokushi|ja|鈴木牧之}}, published in Tenpō 12 (1841)]]
Santō Kyōzan's representative works include ''Fukushū Imoseyama Monogatari'' (''The Tale of Vengeance at Imoseyama,'' 1807) and ''Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki'' (''Old Patterns of Maidenly Fame,'' 1835).<ref>{{kotobank|Mukashi Moyō Musume Hyōbanki|デジタル大辞泉の解説}}</ref><ref>Takagi Gen (trans.), illustrated by {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada|ja|歌川国貞}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kunisada III|ja|歌川国貞 (3代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Kuniteru II|ja|歌川国輝 (2代目)}}, {{ill|Utagawa Yoshitsuna|ja|歌川芳綱}}, and {{ill|Utagawa Kuniyoshi}}. ''Santō Kyōzan: Tales of Eccentric Antiquity'' (Edo Strange Tales and Curiosities Anthology). 2003. ISBN: 978-4336042743.</ref> His lifelong projects include ''Ōmisoka Akebono Zōshi'' (''New Year's Eve Dawn Tales,'' begun in Tenpō 10 (1839)) and ''Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi'' (''Oborozuki's Cat Tales,'' 1842–1849), illustrated by Utagawa Kuniyoshi.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Oborozuki Neko Kusazōshi Vol. 7, 28 volumes|NCID=BB04697476}}. In 2013, translated and republished as ''The Great Adventures of Okoma: Tales of Oborozuki’s Cat'' by Kaneko Nobuhisa. ISBN: 978-4756244291.</ref> He also wrote ''Kyōkusō Nyōbō Kageki'' (''Female Models for Moral Instruction,'' Kōka 3 (1846)), which incorporated moral lessons targeted at women and girls. His essay ''Rekisei Jōsōkō'' (''An Examination of Female Dress Across the Ages,'' Kōka 4 (1847)) remains a valuable historical source on Edo-period customs.
[[File:SantoKyozan20111123.jpg|thumb|200px|Grave of Santō Kyōzan]] He also contributed to the publication of Suzuki Bokushi's ''Hokuetsu Seqqu'' by editing and revising it under the name "Kyōzan Jin Momoki."
